blocks.content:
Section 1
The previous verses talked about not consuming people's property, not giving bribes, not bypassing the system, and focusing on taqwa. Now, in this verse, the atmosphere suddenly changes and we enter the phase of physical and military conflict. It’s as if those previous exercises were prerequisites so that a person doesn't lose control in the most heated and lawless conditions possible, which is war. The hidden message seems to be that someone who can’t restrain their stomach's desires during peace—how can they restrain their anger and survival instinct on the battlefield?

The verse commands battle but immediately limits it. First, it says fighting must be fi sabil Allah (in the way of God), meaning it's not for conquest, taking spoils, or personal grudges. Then it adds a much more important filter: only fight those who are fighting you. The word structure shows that this conflict is a reaction to an ongoing action. This is a completely defensive strategy. You only have the right to draw a weapon on someone who is actually in the phase of fighting you; there is no permission for preemptive strikes or harming people who have nothing to do with you.

The next part of the verse says la ta’tadu (do not transgress). Right in the heart of the most violent human event, where moral boundaries are usually pushed aside, the verse draws a firm red line. This is exactly like a self-defense class. When someone attacks you on the street, you have the right to repel their blow and pin them down until the danger is gone. But once they’re on the ground and no longer a threat, if you stand over them and keep hitting them out of anger, you’ve crossed the line of defense and become the aggressor yourself. Not transgressing in war is the same: not involving civilians, not using unnecessary violence, or not continuing the conflict when the other side has stopped fighting.

The ending of the verse is very decisive, saying God does not love the mu’tadin (transgressors). Notice it doesn't just say God doesn't like it when you transgress; it uses a word that indicates a change in identity. Someone who crosses these red lines in war hasn't just made a simple mistake—their entire identity has changed. They are no longer the person defending themselves and their community in the way of God; they are now labeled a "transgressor." As soon as this new identity is formed, the entire battle and its divine justification collapse and become void. To me, this verse represents a very difficult balance. It allows you to perform the most violent physical act possible to defend yourself, but simultaneously demands the highest level of psychological and moral control.

Section 2
Tense contrast: Present continuous vs. Imperative
In the first part of the verse, qatala is in the mufa'ala form, which is reciprocal. But there is a very important linguistic nuance here regarding timing. The command qatilu (fight) is linked to the present verb yuqatilunakum (those who are fighting you). This means permission to battle is only against those who are continuously and currently fighting you. The verse doesn't say alladhina qatalukum (those who fought you in the past), so the path to seeking revenge for old wounds is completely closed. On the other hand, it doesn't say alladhina sayuqatilunakum (those who are going to fight you), so the idea of a preemptive strike or punishment before a crime is also ruled out. Permission for battle is strictly limited to the present; war is only permitted as long as the other side's war engine is running.

Intentional transgression: The ifta'al form in ta’tadu from the root a-d-w means crossing a limit or boundary. But here, the verb la ta’tadu is in the ifta'al form. This form usually carries the meaning of "deliberateness," "acceptance," and "will." This means the boundary-crossing the verse forbids isn't a simple slip or an unintentional calculation error in the chaos and dust of war. It’s a conscious decision to ignore the rules. When you decide on the battlefield to kill a prisoner or harm civilians, you are crossing the line with your own will, and this verb targets exactly that intentional violation.

The Golden Cross-reference: The dual boundaries of i'tida
I think the most interesting point of this verse shows itself when we compare the final phrase with the rest of the Quran. The exact same combination wa la ta’tadu inna Allaha la yuhibbu al-mu’tadin appears in verse [5:87], but in a completely different context! There it says: "Do not forbid the good things that God has made lawful for you and do not transgress; God does not love the transgressors." This contrast is incredible. Here, i'tida means exceeding the limit in violence and war. There, i'tida means exceeding the limit in asceticism and self-denial by making lawful pleasures forbidden to yourself. By using a consistent structure for both situations, the Quran is showing us that i'tida isn't just about brutality or oppression of others. Any kind of departure from the point of balance—whether it’s an excess of violence or an excess of displayed asceticism—gets the same label of mu’tadin from the system.

Fi Sabil Allah:
The word sabil comes from the root s-b-l and has a subtle difference from words like tariq or sirat, which also mean "way" or "path." Sabil usually refers to smooth, specific paths that have been trodden before and are marked. When it says your battle must be fi sabil Allah, it means this conflict cannot happen in a space of lawlessness and chaos. It must be exactly on that marked path, following the road signs precisely. As soon as you cross the boundaries (ta’tadu), you are no longer on that sabil, so your battle is no longer fi sabil Allah and turns into a personal conflict for venting anger.

Negation of identity with la yuhibbu and inna:
Grammatically, the sentence inna Allaha la yuhibbu al-mu’tadin is a nominal sentence starting with the emphasis particle inna to convey the stability and certainty of this law. The point is that God doesn't say yaghdabu ala al-mu’tadin (He is angry with the transgressors), He says He does not love them. The verb yuhibbu (loves) carries an emotional and connective weight. War is inherently full of violence and tension, but the verse says if you cross the boundaries in that violent space, your connection and love with Me is cut. This means even in the middle of the battlefield, maintaining that emotional connection and link to the system depends on internal control and respecting the red lines.

Part 1
The previous verse was about bribery and seizing others' property using judges. There, God likened the act of the person who gives a bribe to "dropping a bucket into a well," meaning using a hidden path to bypass the law. Now, in this verse, He immediately introduces a new image and says do not enter houses from the back, but come in through the main doors. It seems these two verses are perfectly locked together. Bribery is exactly that—bypassing the natural path and entering through the back door. When you abandon the transparent and legal path and look for a loophole to reach your goal faster, you are essentially punching a hole in the house wall.

The verse starts with a question about the crescent moons (ahillah). The root of hilal comes from raising the voice and shouting with joy (halhala), because when the Arabs saw the new moon, they would shout with happiness and tell each other, showing it was a social phenomenon. It’s interesting that when people ask about the moon, instead of holding an astronomy class and explaining orbital paths, God gives a very practical answer and says these are timers for the people and for hajj. It's as if the Quran shuts down theoretical and marginal questions and shifts focus toward what is useful in the real, daily lives of humans. A natural calendar installed on the wall of the sky so everyone can measure time by it.

Further on, the tone of the verse suddenly changes and says righteousness is not entering houses from the back. Before Islam, it was a custom that when they entered the state of ihram for hajj, they would no longer go through the house door but would jump in from the roof or the back wall, thinking this asceticism was a kind of good deed. The Quran puts its finger exactly on this superstitious behavior and says righteousness is not these strange and weird physical acts at all. Righteousness is in the person who practices taqwa and guards their inner self. Here, just like in [2:177] which said righteousness is not facing East or West, it is once again clearing the concept of "good work" from a meaningless, superficial checklist.

The command "enter the houses through their doors" is probably one of the most beautiful behavioral patterns in the Quran that can be generalized to all of life. God is saying do not unnecessarily complicate natural and straightforward paths for yourselves. Strict, self-invented religiosity is not a virtue. In everything, from learning a skill to human relationships and attaining your rights, enter through that same main door and transparent path, and do not chase illusory shortcuts. The ending of the verse with the word falah shows that salvation and fruition are found exactly in this direct path and with that same constant care, not by finding a secret way and a back door.

Part 2
Here we have one of the strangest and most fascinating syntactic shifts in the entire Quran, which we saw exactly before in [2:177]. The word birr (righteousness and expansiveness in goodness) is a completely abstract concept. According to linguistic rules, we would expect the sentence to say "righteousness is that you practice taqwa" (an tattaqu). But the verse breaks this composition and says: "but birr is the one who has taqwa" (wa lakinna al-birra man ittaqa). That is, it has equated an abstract concept with a "human" (man).

Classical commentators like Zamakhshari, when they reach this structure, usually say there is a hidden word (a missing madaf) and it was originally "righteousness is the righteousness of the one who...". But if we remain faithful to the text itself and accept this strange equality, a much deeper layer of meaning opens up. I think the Quran is emphasizing that virtues are not checklists of physical acts (like which door you enter through). Virtue is authentic when it is manifested in a human and becomes one with them. The person with taqwa himself becomes the very essence of righteousness.

An Auditory Root for a Visual Phenomenon
The word ahillah (plural of hilal) comes from the root h-l-l. In etymological study, this word is not originally visual at all, but entirely auditory. It means raising the voice and shouting. In the society of that day, when the new moon was seen in the sky, people would shout out of joy so that others would also know and the new month would begin. The placement of this root exactly next to the word "for the people" (li-n-nas) tells us that the lunar calendar in that context was not just an astronomical phenomenon, but a social and networked event that synced and aligned people's lives with one another.

The Contrast of Zuhur and Abwab in the Geometry of Behavior
The root z-h-r refers to the back of anything (a place hidden from view or where entry is unnatural), in contrast to b-w-b which is the natural, transparent, and defined place of entry. The interesting part is that in the previous verse [2:188], there was a discussion of bribery and consuming ill-gotten wealth, and we had the verb tudlu (dropping a bucket into the darkness of a well to pull up benefit). Here, we have "coming from the back of the houses." Both are describing a corrupt behavioral pattern: bypassing the system, lack of transparency, and using unnatural, dark paths to reach a goal.

Agriculture and Falah
The verse ends with the phrase "so that you may attain falah" (la’allakum tuflihun). The root f-l-h in its basic meaning means splitting the earth and plowing (which is why a farmer is called a fallah). The choice of this word in a verse that begins with the measurement of time and seasons (mawaqit) and continues with the rules of entering through the right path (abwab) is very precise. A farmer, to reach fruition, must both know the timing and rhythm of nature and must perform the tasks through the correct path and rule. Salvation (falah) in this view is not a magical event; like farming, it requires coordination with the system, transparency in the path, and constant care.
In the video I recorded for [2:5], I mistakenly linked falah to the splitting of the lip during a happy smile. Although it is a beautiful interpretation, it is not supported by etymology.

طيب.note: Pleasantness, purity, being healthy, and being useful

رزق.note: Typically, it carries the connotation that *rizq* is something that comes from God. It includes food but is not limited to food. Knowledge, children, power, and various types of provisions all fall under the umbrella of *rizq*.

شكر.note:
In pre-Islamic Arabic, it was used for a camel that produced abundant milk with little grazing.
Its imagery is a kind of active, fruitful response to what has been received. Like an amplifier: you take an input and multiply it several times over.
